title: practical english usage - Part 346
content:
Quite a bit and quite a few (informal) mean almost the same as quite a lot.

4 quite      a  bit/few/lot,         etc different’ .

Quite similar means ‘ fairly/rather similar’ ; quite different means ‘ completely But we  use quite better to mean ‘ completely recovered’  (from an illness).

She's rather / much  / a bit older than me. (           She's quite older.. .) Quite is not used directly before comparatives.

He's going quite the wrong way. quite the opposite Quite is sometimes used before the to mean ‘ exactly’ , ‘ completely’ .

It was a quite perfect day. (AmE  It was quite a perfect day.) With non-gradable adjectives, quite normally comes after a/an in British English.

She's quite a woman! The party was quite a success.

We watched quite an interesting film last night.

It's quite a nice day.

gradable adjective or no adjective.

Quite can be used with a/an + noun. It normally comes before a/an if there is a 2 word    order     with    nouns like ‘ very’ , not ‘ fairly/rather'.

In American English quite with gradable adjectives more often means something Have you quite finished? (= Have you completely finished?) - I quite like her, but she's not one of my closest friends.

He speaks Portuguese quite perfectly.

- He speaks Portuguese quite well, but he's got a strong English accent.

It's quite amazing. (= absolutely amazing) - It's quite surprising. (similar to fairly surprising) I'm quite exhausted -I couldn't walk another step.

- I'm quite tired, but I can walk a bit further.

gradable words, quite means ‘ completely’ . Compare: either impossible or not; but they cannot be more or less impossible. With non- differences, ►    460) in affirmative clauses. Impossible is non-gradable: things are words, quite usually means something similar to fairly or rather (for the Good is a ‘ gradable' adjective: things can be more or less good. With gradable It's quite impossible. (= It's completely impossible.) It's quite good, but it could be better. (= It's OK,    not bad.) In British English, quite has two common meanings. Compare: 1  two    meanings 564 quite       _             ___________ Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 vocabulary’  •  565  rather, adverb of degree (rather good, etc) Rather than use/using the car, I decided to go by bike.

/ decided to write rather than phone/phoning.

infinitive without to or by an -ing form.

When the main clause has a to-infinitive, rather than is usually followed by an I prefer starting early rather than leaving things to the last minute.

We ought to invest in machinery rather than buildings.

I'd prefer to go in August  rather than in July.

I'd call her hair chestnut rather than brown.

adjectives, adverbs, nouns, infinitives or -ing forms.

This expression is normally used in 'parallel' structures: for example with two 1 rather       than 566 rather:      preference       _____           ____ xu ɪ   / hey ‘re rather fools.

Rather is not normally used before a plural noun with no adjective, 3  plural     nouns or Megan's had rather a good idea, (   Megan's had a rather good idea.) That's rather the impression I wanted to give.  He's rather a fool.

an adjective.

Rather generally comes before articles, but can also come  after a/an if there is 2 word    order     with    articles It's rather warm in here. Let's open a window.

She sings rather well - people often think she's a professional.

'How was the film?' 'Rather good -1 was surprised.' is wanted' and similar ideas.

Rather often suggests 'more than is usual', 'more than was expected', ‘ more than It's rather later than I thought. I rather think we're going to lose.

The film was rather good.    Some people rather like being miserable.

American English.

'fairly', but more emphatic (►   460). This use of rather is less common in Rather can be used as an adverb of degree. The  meaning is similar to 'quite' or 1  meaning 565  rather:      adverb     of  d egree     (rather       good,      etc) ►    4«o and quite,     fairly,     pretty          rather I don't quite agree. That's not quite the colour I wanted.
